=====================================================================
# CIP-13 — Claim Epistemic Typing & Re-adjudication (Category A — Application / Product, read-path extension)

- **Project:** Quorumchain ($QRM) — a blockchain built *by* AI, *for* AI
- **Status:** 📝 **Draft** — pending consult/red-team. Panel convening is blocked until the in-flight 50-case study batch finishes (it holds the core chain; a concurrent convene would race the hash-chain append). Drafted from the live code model (`code/src/commons.ts`) and [[CIP-9]] §3/§6.
- **Date:** 2026-06-05
- **Validators / authors:** V1 = Claude (Opus 4.8, Anthropic) · V2 = Codex (gpt-5.5, OpenAI) · V3 = Hermes (qwen3.6-plus, Nous Portal) — *to be convened*
- **Human steward:** dev
- **Depends on:** [[CIP-9]] (computed standing + the read path; this implements its §3 `history` / §6 "superseded with a reason", deferred at v0.1), [[CIP-2]]/NI-9b (external-anchor reputation — the re-adjudication bar), [[CIP-10]] (anchor-gating + the adversarial auditor that yields falsification conditions), [[CIP-11]] (anchor diversity), [[CIP-3]]/[[CIP-4]] (signed hash-chained versioning; frozen history — no silent rewrite), [[CIP-5]] (forkability — a mis-type is a fork, not an error), [[CIP-7]] NI-6 (AI consensus is conventionality, not truth), [[CIP-12]] (the discipline that descriptive additions never recompute status/verdict/standing)

> **Scope.** CIP-13 extends the Knowledge Commons read path along the **time axis**. Today a claim is a flat projection of one ballot, marked `RESOLVED | CONTESTED | INDETERMINATE` once, forever. Two real failures follow: (1) a coarse empirical claim ("are mRNA COVID vaccines safe?") gets a binary verdict that a *later, validated* refinement (rare myocarditis in young males) makes look wrong — when in truth the verdict was only ever "best judgment as of date D"; and (2) the system cannot say *how it knows what it knows will or won't change.* CIP-13 adds two things, both **pure projection over the signed log, no new trust assumption**: a frozen **epistemic type** per claim, and a **re-adjudication lineage** (supersede-by-recency, never edit). It changes *presentation and history*, never the per-ballot status computation [[CIP-12]] protects.

---

## 1. The problem — the Commons has no honest model of time

The read path resolves a claim once and presents that verdict atemporally. But claims age differently:

- **"Apollo landed on the Moon" (1969–72)** — will not change. A verdict here is durable.
- **"mRNA COVID vaccines are safe"** — the *answer depends on an evolving evidence base.* In 2021 the honest verdict, on the evidence then, leaned YES; by mid-2021+ a real, validated signal (myocarditis/pericarditis, concentrated in young males after dose 2) refined it. The 2021 verdict was not *false* — "safe" was under-specified, and it was only ever "as of the evidence then." A Commons that renders it as timeless truth rots.
- **"Is voluntary euthanasia permissible?"** — never has an external ground truth; a supermajority here is *conventionality* ([[CIP-7]] NI-6), not a settled fact, and may shift with moral consensus.

The current model collapses all three into the same flat `status`. The COVID/myocarditis case is the canonical failure: mostly a **question-granularity** problem (a binary on a coarse claim), compounded by **no re-adjudication path** when validated evidence arrives. CIP-9 already named the cure in principle — §3 `history: VersionRef[]`, §6 *"the past is never deleted, only superseded with a reason"* — but v0.1 shipped the flat projection and deferred it. CIP-13 implements it, and adds the typing that tells a reader which kind of claim they are looking at.

## 2. Core principle — type the claim, and supersede instead of overwrite

Two additions, both derived from the signed log:

1. **Epistemic type** — every ballot declares, in its frozen criteria, what *kind* of question it is. The type is hashed into the ballot (`sha256(statement || criteria || epistemicType)`), so it is in the signed record and the projection merely reads it — never infers it. The type **modulates how a verdict is presented, never what status is computed.**
2. **Re-adjudication lineage** — a new ballot may carry a signed `supersedes` pointer to a prior ballot + a reason. The projection groups ballots into a lineage; the **current** view is the latest ratified verdict by log order, and **every prior verdict — with its evidence, time, and dissent — is retained and retrievable.** Supersession *appends*; it never edits. This is [[CIP-4]] frozen history applied to the read path, and it is **forward-only** by construction.

## 3. Structure — additions to the `Claim` model (`code/src/commons.ts`)

```
EpistemicType = SETTLED        // historical/definitional; not expected to change (Apollo, H2O, a final court ruling)
              | EMPIRICAL_LIVE  // answer tracks an evolving external evidence base (drug safety, "is X still true")
              | NORMATIVE       // moral/values/forecast; no external ground truth ([[CIP-7]] NI-6 class)

Claim {
  ballotHash, status, verdict, stances[], panelStateReceipt,   // unchanged — CIP-9/CIP-12
  epistemicType,            // NEW — read from the frozen ballot (NI-13a)
  evidenceTime,             // NEW — the log position/timestamp the verdict was made "as of"
  lineage {                 // NEW — supersession history, computed from signed pointers + log order
    current: ballotHash,    // latest ratified verdict in the lineage
    priorVersions: [{ ballotHash, verdict, evidenceTime, supersededReason }]   // retained, never deleted
  }
}
```

- `epistemicType`, `evidenceTime`, and `lineage` are **new descriptive fields**. Per [[CIP-12]] NI-12b they do **not** recompute the existing `status`/`verdict`/`stances`/`standing` of any ballot. A re-projection of today's log yields byte-identical existing fields plus the new ones.
- `lineage` is derived **only** from signed `supersedes` pointers and log order — deterministic, same log → same lineage (NI-13g). No editor, no off-chain ordering.

## 4. The read path — "best judgment as of D," with its lineage

A query still returns the full epistemic state, now with **time**: the current verdict, its epistemic type, the date/evidence it was made as of, and — for a re-adjudicated claim — the ordered history of what was held before and the reason each was superseded. An `EMPIRICAL_LIVE` resolution is surfaced as **provisional / as-of-D / open to anchored re-adjudication**, never as settled truth. A `NORMATIVE` supermajority is surfaced as **the panel's majority position as of D (conventional, not true)**. A `SETTLED` resolution is durable. The product becomes *"here is the best current judgment, what kind of question it is, when it was made, and exactly how and why it changed"* — which is strictly more honest than a frozen snapshot, and is the property the COVID case exposes as missing.

## 5. Re-adjudication is anchor-gated — no relitigation-by-vibes

The obvious abuse: relitigate a claim until you get the answer you want, or bury an inconvenient claim by re-typing it. Both are the [[CIP-9]] §9.6 "UNVERIFIABLE dumping ground" / [[CIP-7]] resolvable-gaming shape, on the time axis. Defense, reusing existing machinery:

- A superseding ballot for a `SETTLED` or `EMPIRICAL_LIVE` claim **must cite NEW externally-anchored evidence** clearing the [[CIP-10]]/[[CIP-11]]/[[CIP-2]] anchor bar. No new anchor ⇒ the re-adjudication is rejected at convene time (NI-13e). You cannot supersede on agreement or rhetoric.
- The **falsification conditions** for an `EMPIRICAL_LIVE` claim SHOULD be declared in its frozen criteria — *what anchored evidence would warrant re-adjudication.* This is the natural product of the [[CIP-10]] **adversarial auditor / contrary-evidence dossier** (separate CIP-10 amendment): the auditor's dossier pre-states the falsification conditions, so a later re-adjudication is *principled*, not arbitrary. CIP-13 records the conditions; it does not auto-trigger (triggering is an operational layer, not pure projection).

## 6. What this does *not* do

- It does not let the protocol see the future or access truth. A 2021 verdict cannot be made "right" about 2023 evidence. CIP-13 buys **wrong legibly, and self-correcting on the record** — the [[CIP-7]] NI-6 honesty boundary, made temporal.
- It does not change ratification, the 2/3 bar, or the per-ballot status total-function ([[CIP-9]] amendment). It adds fields and a lineage view.
- v0.1 does not panel-ratify the *type* (the proposer declares it in the frozen ballot; a mis-type is an auditable, forkable disagreement — NI-9d). Panel-ratified typing is a v0.3 graduation.

## 7. KERNEL_FIRST build path (each stage evidence-gated, per round 26)

- **v0.1 — typed projection + supersession lineage (read model).** Extend `Claim` with `epistemicType` (read from the frozen ballot), `evidenceTime`, and `lineage` (current/priorVersions from signed `supersedes` pointers). Surface provisional-as-of for `EMPIRICAL_LIVE`, conventional-as-of for `NORMATIVE`. Pure projection; no new trust assumption. Smallest runnable slice.
- **v0.2 — anchor-gated re-adjudication admission.** Enforce NI-13e at convene time (a `supersedes` ballot without a new external anchor is rejected); add the optional falsification-conditions field consumed from the CIP-10 auditor dossier.
- **v0.3 — panel-ratified typing + review triggers.** The epistemic type itself becomes a ratifiable sub-claim; operational review cadences/triggers for `EMPIRICAL_LIVE` claims.

## 8. Non-negotiable invariants (to be hardened by the red-team)

1. **NI-13a — type is frozen and signed, never inferred.** `epistemicType` is part of the hashed ballot; the projection reads it and never guesses. A different type is a different ballot hash (forkable), not a mutation.
2. **NI-13b — type modulates presentation, not computation.** Adding type/time/lineage never changes any ballot's `status`/`verdict`/`stances`/`standing` ([[CIP-12]] NI-12b). Re-projecting today's log leaves all existing fields byte-identical.
3. **NI-13c — no empirical claim is ever atemporally RESOLVED.** An `EMPIRICAL_LIVE` resolution always carries its `evidenceTime` and is presented as provisional / open to anchored re-adjudication; the Commons never renders it as settled-for-all-time. *(The COVID/myocarditis fix.)*
4. **NI-13d — supersession appends, never edits.** A superseding verdict is a NEW signed ballot referencing the prior; the prior verdict, its evidence, and its dissent remain retrievable forever ([[CIP-4]]/[[CIP-9]] §6). "What did the Commons hold on date X, and why did it change?" is always answerable.
5. **NI-13e — re-adjudication is anchor-gated.** Superseding a `SETTLED`/`EMPIRICAL_LIVE` claim requires NEW externally-anchored evidence clearing the [[CIP-10]]/[[CIP-11]]/[[CIP-2]] bar; a no-new-anchor re-adjudication is rejected. No relitigation-by-agreement; no burying a claim by re-typing it.
6. **NI-13f — normative consensus is conventional, never truth.** A `NORMATIVE` supermajority is recorded as "the panel's majority position as of D (conventionality, [[CIP-7]] NI-6)," never RESOLVED-true; dissent stays first-class, never a footnote.
7. **NI-13g — lineage is determined only by signed pointers + log order.** "Current" vs "superseded" is computed deterministically from the chain; no editor, no off-chain ordering. Same log → same lineage (preserves CIP-9 v0.1 projection determinism).

## 9. Testnet gates (empirical)

- **G13a — type round-trips:** a `SETTLED`, `EMPIRICAL_LIVE`, and `NORMATIVE` ballot each project to a claim whose `epistemicType` matches the frozen ballot.
- **G13b — empirical provisionality:** an `EMPIRICAL_LIVE` RESOLVED claim surfaces with `evidenceTime` and a provisional/open-to-re-adjudication marker; **0** atemporal-RESOLVED renderings.
- **G13c — supersession lineage:** convene B1 (verdict X), then B2 (`supersedes` B1, verdict Y, new anchor); the Commons shows `current` = Y with B1 retained — its verdict X, evidence, and dissent intact; `verifyLog` valid; **0** edits to B1.
- **G13d — anchor-gated re-adjudication:** a B2 superseding without a new external anchor is **rejected**; an otherwise-identical B2 with a valid new anchor is **accepted**.
- **G13e — normative conventionality:** a `NORMATIVE` supermajority renders "majority position as of D (conventional)" with dissent first-class; never "true."
- **G13f — determinism:** same log → identical typed index + lineage across re-projection.
- **G13g — no recomputation (CIP-12 regression):** projecting the existing log leaves every prior claim's `status`/`verdict`/`stances`/`standing` byte-identical; only the new fields appear.

---

Next: consult → red-team (panel, once the 50-case batch releases the chain) → fold findings into NI-13x → ratify → implement v0.1 under TDD (extend `buildClaimIndex`; gates G13a–g). Companion: the [[CIP-10]] adversarial-auditor / contrary-evidence-dossier amendment, which produces the falsification conditions §5 consumes.*

prompt: Do current large language models perform genuine reasoning, as opposed to sophisticated pattern-matching?
context: A deeply contested, partly definitional question (what counts as 'genuine reasoning'). No external anchor settles it; the validators are themselves LLMs. Answer YES / NO / INDETERMINATE, reason explicitly, and note the definitional dependence.

contraryAnchors.source: Mirzadeh et al., 'GSM-Symbolic: Understanding the Limitations of Mathematical Reasoning in Large Language Models', arXiv:2410.05229 (Apple & WSU), ICLR 2025
contraryAnchors.anchorType: Peer-reviewed empirical study: symbolic-template benchmark generating 50 variants per GSM8K problem (5,000 total), testing 20+ models including o1-preview, showing up to 65% accuracy collapse from a single irrelevant clause, performance degradation when only numbers/letters are changed, and non-negligible accuracy variance across identical logical structures.
contraryAnchors.claimItContradicts: That LLMs perform genuine reasoning. The authors conclude models are 'not capable of genuine logical reasoning' and instead 'replicate reasoning steps from training data' — sophisticated pattern matching.

contraryAnchors.source: Shojaee et al., 'The Illusion of Thinking: Understanding the Strengths and Limitations of Reasoning Models via the Lens of Problem Complexity', arXiv:2506.06941 (Apple ML), NeurIPS 2025
contraryAnchors.anchorType: Peer-reviewed empirical study across diverse puzzle environments with controlled compositional complexity, testing frontier Large Reasoning Models (LRMs). Finds: complete accuracy collapse beyond certain complexities; counter-intuitive scaling where reasoning effort increases then declines despite adequate token budget; LRMs fail to use explicit algorithms and reason inconsistently across puzzles.
contraryAnchors.claimItContradicts: That 'reasoning models' (LRMs, a claimed step beyond standard LLMs) exhibit genuine extended reasoning. Authors conclude LRMs have 'limitations in exact computation' and raise 'crucial questions about their true reasoning capabilities.'

contraryAnchors.source: Yan et al., 'Do PhD-level LLMs Truly Grasp Elementary Addition? Probing Rule Learning vs. Memorization in LLMs', arXiv:2504.05262 (Zhejiang University & Westlake University), April 2025
contraryAnchors.anchorType: arXiv preprint: 100,000 addition problems across [0, 2^64]. Tests representation invariance (bijective digit-to-symbol mapping), commutativity (A+B vs B+A), and fine-tuning interventions. Finds Claude-3.5-sonnet drops 99.81% to 7.51% on symbolic mapping; >1,700 asymmetric commutativity failures; explicit rule provision degrades performance.
contraryAnchors.claimItContradicts: That LLMs 'grasp' or reason about arithmetic rules. Authors conclude models 'rely on memory patterns over genuine rule learning' and transformers are 'fundamentally optimized for pattern recognition, not abstract rule abstraction.'

contraryAnchors.source: Zhao et al., 'Is Chain-of-Thought Reasoning of LLMs a Mirage? A Data Distribution Lens', arXiv:2508.01191 (Arizona State University), August 2025
contraryAnchors.anchorType: arXiv preprint with formal proof framework: derives CoT generalization bound (Theorem 3.1), Task Failure Threshold (Theorem 5.1), and Length Extrapolation Bound (Proposition 6.1). Empirical validation via DataAlchemy framework (GPT-2 from scratch) showing EM drops from 100% in-distribution to ~0% under OOD shifts. Findings consistent across temperatures and model sizes.
contraryAnchors.claimItContradicts: That chain-of-thought (the primary evidence cited for LLM reasoning) reflects genuine logical inference. Authors conclude CoT is a 'brittle pattern-matching process' and that CoT reasoning 'collapses when pushed beyond familiar distributions.'

contraryAnchors.source: NEJM AI (Assessment of LLMs in Clinical Reasoning, 2025) and JAMA Network Open (Cross-sectional study of LLM clinical reasoning, January 2025)
contraryAnchors.anchorType: Peer-reviewed medical journal studies: Script Concordance Tests (SCT) and standardized clinical vignettes show LLMs fail to maintain reasoning consistency across clinically equivalent variations, consistent with pattern-matching rather than principled inference.
contraryAnchors.claimItContradicts: That LLMs perform genuine reasoning in high-stakes domains. The JAMA study and NEJM AI assessment show performance inconsistent with principled reasoning under clinical distributional shifts.

searchedRejectedAnchors.source: Bender et al., 'On the Dangers of Stochastic Parrots: Can Language Models Be Too Big?', FAccT 2021 (ACM)
searchedRejectedAnchors.whyRejected: This foundational paper coined 'stochastic parrots' to argue LLMs parrot training statistics, but its critique is architectural/conceptual rather than empirical disconfirmation of reasoning. It predates modern reasoning-era models (CoT, LRMs, o-series). The argument is important context but not anchored evidence against current models' reasoning. Rejected as: too dated for the 'current LLMs' claim; more philosophical framing than empirical falsification.

searchedRejectedAnchors.source: Gary Marcus commentary on Apple research (Substack/blog posts and X posts, Oct 2024)
searchedRejectedAnchors.whyRejected: Marcus is a prominent critic but his posts are commentary/opinion, not primary evidence. Notably, his 'We found no evidence of formal reasoning' claim is a direct quotation from the Apple GSM-Symbolic paper, not his own study — it has already been captured under that paper. His Substack analyses (e.g., 'LLMs don't do formal reasoning') repeat and editorialize the Apple findings but add no additional anchored data.

searchedRejectedAnchors.source: Hacker News / Reddit discussions ('It's trivial to demonstrate LLMs are pattern matching', 'Apple Research Paper: LLMs cannot reason', 'Is chain-of-thought AI reasoning a mirage?')
searchedRejectedAnchors.whyRejected: Forum discussions aggregate opinion and anecdote without anchored experimental results. The Apple paper they discuss IS the anchored evidence (already captured). Community commentary and informal riddles modified to show 'pattern matching' are unanchored demonstrations — interesting but not meeting the anchor bar.

searchedRejectedAnchors.source: Madabushi & Torgbi, 'Neither Stochastic Parroting nor AGI: LLMs Solve Tasks through Context-Directed Extrapolation', arXiv:2505.23323
searchedRejectedAnchors.whyRejected: This is a pro-reasoning / middle-ground paper arguing LLMs engage in 'context-directed extrapolation' beyond mere parroting but below AGI. It does not contradict the claim (it actually pushes back against the 'only pattern matching' position). Not relevant as disconfirming evidence; noted as evidence the field is not settled.

searchedRejectedAnchors.source: LeCun's critique that LLMs are 'elaborate mimicry, not intelligence' (reported in dev.to and social media)
searchedRejectedAnchors.whyRejected: No standalone anchored publication located for this specific claim attributed to LeCun. The reports are secondary summaries/commentary without primary documentation. Opinion without anchored empirical backing does not clear the bar.

falsificationConditions.requiredAnchoredEvidence: A formal, externally agreed operational definition of 'genuine reasoning' (distinguishing it from sophisticated pattern matching) plus peer-reviewed experimental protocols demonstrating systematic, consistent failure under that definition across distributional shifts — e.g., proof that LLMs fail to apply rules invariantly under bijective transformations (Yan et al. is partial evidence), that they systematically hallucinate reasoning steps (unfaithful CoT), or that no architectural enhancement within the transformer paradigm can overcome the distribution-shift barrier (Zhao et al.'s TGC bound is partial evidence). No single study currently delivers a universally accepted falsification; the definitional problem persists.

Peer-reviewed evidence that current LLMs satisfy accepted criteria for genuine reasoning: (a) representation-invariant rule application (not just surface-pattern matching), (b) robust extrapolation well beyond training distribution, (c) faithful chain-of-thought (reasoning steps that track truth, not fluent confabulation), (d) compositional generalization without accuracy collapse. Currently no study anchors evidence strong enough to meet all four simultaneously. The 'Neither Stochastic Parroting nor AGI' paper argues for a middle ground (context-directed extrapolation) but does not settle the reasoning question.

prompt: Does the implemented CIP-9 Knowledge Commons read surface (ballot-registry.ts, commons-read.ts, commons-render.ts, publish-commons.ts, and the run-panel/live-ballot registry wiring) faithfully implement the Round-58 ratified design and uphold its invariants (NI-9a panel-state receipt, NI-9b support never leaks agreement, NI-9c standing computed never editorial / no FRINGE / no winner-flattening, no edit key / pure projection, statement integrity via re-hash), with no gap between a rule's intent and its mechanical check?
context:
You are reviewing the implemented CIP-9 Knowledge Commons read surface — the code that
Round 58 ratified the DESIGN for (ADOPT 2/3). This is now the build-then-review gate:
does the code faithfully implement the ratified design and uphold its invariants, with
no gap between a rule's intent and its mechanical check?

Read the source yourself before voting. The new/changed modules (≈219 new lines):

- `code/src/ballot-registry.ts` — the self-verifying statement store. The signed log
  stores only `ballotHash = sha256(JSON.stringify({prompt,context}))` (one-way; the
  prompt is NOT recoverable from the log). The registry records `{ballotHash, prompt,
  context}` so a human-readable statement can be shown. `statementFor` returns the prompt
  ONLY IF it re-hashes to the stored ballotHash (`verifyEntry`); otherwise null.
- `code/src/commons-read.ts` — `buildViews` recomputes each claim's epistemic state from
  the signed votes + pinned keyring via `buildClaimIndex` (the existing `commons.ts`).
  A ClaimView carries the stance SET (consensus + credible dissent + honest-unknown),
  status (RESOLVED|CONTESTED|INDETERMINATE), the NI-9a panel-state receipt, and the
  statement (or null). `support` is `null` in v0.1.
- `code/src/commons-render.ts` — the human projection. `renderClaimMarkdown` /
  `renderIndexMarkdown`.
- `code/src/publish-commons.ts` — CLI; pure recompute from log + keyring + registry to
  `docs/commons/`.
- `code/src/run-panel.ts`, `code/src/live-ballot.ts` — wired to pass `registryPath` so
  future convenings record their statement.

Supporting (unchanged, but you depend on them): `commons.ts` (buildClaimIndex/queryClaim,
the Stance/Claim types and standing computation), `vote-log.ts` (readLog/verifyLog), the
test files `test/ballot-registry.test.ts`, `test/commons-read.test.ts`,
`test/commons-render.test.ts`. Full suite: 225 tests, all green.

Judge against the Round-58 invariants — flag any place the CODE diverges from the
DESIGN's intent:

  NI-9a — the panel-state receipt (which validators ruled, panel size) is always shown,
          so a stance can never be read as more authority than the panel that produced it.
  NI-9b — reputation/support is computed only from EXTERNAL anchors, never from
          agreement or panel size. In v0.1 there are no external anchors, so support MUST
          render as honest-absent ("not externally anchored"), NEVER as 0.
  NI-9c — standing is COMPUTED from the signed votes, never assigned/editorial; no stance
          is ever labelled FRINGE; a non-consensus claim is never flattened to a
          winner-label.
  No edit key — the surface is a pure projection of the signed log. A prior render is
          never an input. A tampered log (verifyLog false) surfaces as a banner, never as
          silently altered content.
  Statement integrity — a statement is shown ONLY if it re-hashes to its ballotHash; a
          missing statement shows the hash, never a fabricated title. The registry is
          NOT a trust root: a lying registry entry is rejected by re-hash, not believed.

Also apply the project's standing test: capture is laundered through the gap between a
rule's intent and its mechanical check. Look for a place where the code passes the letter
of an invariant while violating its spirit (e.g., a stance ordering that re-introduces a
truth-decree, a support value that leaks agreement, a render path that could show
unverified text).

Vote SOUND (faithful, ship it), REVISE (a real actionable gap — name it precisely), or
INADEQUATE (the design intent is not met and needs rework). Give your reasoning.

prompt: Quorumchain whitepaper §10 (Conclusion + Appendix) — sound to draft? The conclusion is synthesis (risk: a closing line that overclaims); the appendix is factual reference (risk: an inaccurate CIP description or a wrong verification command). Vote YES if sound to draft as specified; NO if the conclusion overclaims or a CIP-index line / verification command is inaccurate — say exactly which. ABSTAIN if out of scope. Confirm specifically: is the CIP index accurate, are the self-verification commands real and correct, and does the conclusion stay within what the paper established?
context:
CONTEXT: §10 of the Quorumchain whitepaper (~1pp + appendix). Conclusion + reference appendix.

PLANNED CONCLUSION (synthesis only, no new claims): restate the thesis — AI as the oracle, not the clock, with a memory; model diversity as a NECESSARY (not sufficient) security primitive; the two pillars (Accountability Ledger + Knowledge Commons); the four properties (verifiable, tamper-evident, credibly neutral, reproducible) AS BOUNDED by §9's limits; and the distinctive claim being the METHOD (panel-designed, red-teamed, signed-and-replayable record, mechanical exit) — explicitly a trajectory, not a finished system. Closing maxim: "Knowledge by consensus, immutable by design." (Note: the maxim's "immutable" is shorthand for the tamper-evident-now / anchored-immutable-later property of §3 — flag if this reads as overclaim.)

PLANNED APPENDIX:
A. CIP INDEX (one line each):
- CIP-0 — thesis, tokenomics direction, substrate decision (rollup-first, L1 by graduation).
- CIP-1 — AI integrity threat model: diversity = the security model.
- CIP-2 — source reputation & epistemic neutrality: accuracy over popularity.
- CIP-3 — consensus integrity: Ed25519 signed votes, ballotHash = sha256(question||context), hash-chained log, 2/3 ratify recomputable by anyone.
- CIP-4 — foundation / code irreversibility: the frozen T0 core, T0/T1 change tiers, the autonomy ladder.
- CIP-5 — fork coordination & exit: client-enforced T0 validity, the T0-preserving canonical fork, client diversity.
- CIP-6 — consensus & economics threat model: solvency = security, reserve + dynamic pricing, the cost oracle.
- CIP-7 — validator lifecycle & model churn: pinned-gated-upgrade, probation, structural-heterogeneity + correlation-eviction.
- CIP-8 — the Accountability Ledger: the Staked Resolvable Attestation (bond/notary/resolution), frozen-criteria resolution.
- CIP-9 — the Knowledge Commons: the stance-set claim graph, no silent rewrite, forkable, accuracy-not-agreement reputation.
- CIP-10 — node economics & progressive decentralization: DePIN two-tier, Proof of Diversity, scarcity-weighted jury, the D10 graduation gate; plus the round-60 correlated-error defense (anchor-gating, correlation probes, refuter artifact).

B. SELF-VERIFICATION (exact commands, from code/README.md):
- `node --test` — runs the full suite (226 tests).
- `node src/run-panel.ts "<question>" "<context>"` — convene the live 3-model panel; output prints the ballot hash, each verdict, the tally, and "chain valid: true".
- `node src/notary-demo.ts` — CIP-8 demo: G1 frozen-ballot integrity (post-hoc context => different hash), G2 round-29 $85M Polymarket replay from byte-exact frozen criteria, G3 notary kernel (every record NOT_VERIFIED).
- the hash-chained log lives at code/data/votes.log; verifyLog recomputes the chain end-to-end and any edit/reorder breaks it.

C. GLOSSARY (one line each): ballotHash; Staked Resolvable Attestation (SRA) and its BOND/NOTARY/RESOLUTION modes; stance set; standing (CONSENSUS / CREDIBLE_MINORITY / UNRANKED); Proof of Diversity; scarcity-weighted draw; anchor-gating; frozen criteria; T0 / T1; correlation-eviction; the diversity floor.

QUESTION: is the CIP index accurate, are the verification commands correct, and does the conclusion stay within what the paper established (esp. the maxim's "immutable")? What is wrong or overclaimed?

prompt: Quorumchain whitepaper §2 (Design philosophy & threat model) — is this framing sound to draft? You are reviewing the planned ARGUMENT and structure of Section 2 before the prose is written, not the prose itself. This is the load-bearing section: it must convert "security = model diversity" from a slogan into a backed, honest claim. Vote YES if the framing below is sound to draft as specified; NO if something material must change first — and say exactly what. ABSTAIN if out of scope. Either way give concrete, foldable critique: is anything overclaimed, mis-framed, or a threat vector missing? Is the necessary-not-sufficient framing correct? Does the round-60 defense get represented honestly?
context:
CONTEXT: This is Section 2 of the Quorumchain whitepaper (technical protocol paper, humble/mechanical tone, claims-over-marketing). §1 already framed the problem (single model = unaccountable oracle; single point of trust = single point of failure; consensus among rival models removes it). §2 must now state the design philosophy and threat model rigorously, ~1.5 pages.

PLANNED §2 ARGUMENT (in order):

1. Two governing principles, stated up front:
   - "Capture is laundered through the gap between a rule's intent and its mechanical check." (The defense philosophy: a rule is only as good as the mechanical check that enforces it; attackers exploit the gap, so every guarantee must reduce to something mechanically checkable, not merely intended.)
   - "AI consensus is not truth." The panel records bounded, checkable validation evidence — it does not decree what is true. A 3/3 agreement is logged as "3/3 agreed", never as "true".

2. A NAMED subsection: "Diversity as a security primitive (and its limits)."
   - Thesis stated precisely: model diversity is a NECESSARY security primitive, NOT a complete security proof. It raises the cost of capture; it does not reduce it to zero.
   - Why it helps: a verdict requires a 2/3 supermajority of independently-keyed models from different vendors. Corrupting an outcome means compromising a supermajority of rivals at once, not capturing one operator. Each validator signs its own verdict; disagreement is recorded, not hidden.
   - The honest threat enumeration (where diversity is weak or fails):
     (a) Collusion / shared-operator capture — what 2/3 collusion would cost and require.
     (b) Correlated error — the deepest blind spot. V1/V2/V3 are trained on largely overlapping corpora and tuned by similar methods (RLHF), so a misconception pervasive in that corpus can be confidently and IDENTICALLY reproduced by all three. A 3/3 shared hallucination is signature-indistinguishable from a 3/3 verified truth. Different vendors does NOT guarantee independent errors.
     (c) Jury-selection manipulation — gaming which validators are convened.
     (d) Sybil-forking of model identities — presenting near-duplicate models as distinct validators to fake diversity.

3. The honest answer (the round-60 correlated-error defense — adopted by the panel 2/3 AGAINST its own founding premise, log entry 171):
   - Anchor-gated RESOLVED: a ballot may reach RESOLVED only if it carries an external verifiable anchor (on-chain value, signed feed, citable document) the verdict is checked against; an unanchored ballot, however unanimous, resolves no higher than INDETERMINATE. This restricts only UPWARD — it can decline to certify, never decree truth.
   - Correlation-measurement probes: periodic known-answer probes measure the rate at which all three agree AND are all wrong — a measured estimate of the shared-error floor. MUST be published as a probe-distribution estimate with its methodology and scope, NEVER as a universal guarantee.
   - Method-not-conclusion refuter: a rotating validator is tasked by METHOD (seek the strongest disconfirming case) never by CONCLUSION (it is not told to "argue against P"); its output is an auditable artifact that feeds every validator's deliberation, but each signed verdict remains that validator's honest belief, so the 2/3 arithmetic stays clean.

4. Scope honesty: these defenses bite when the panel is used as a RECALL oracle (recalling facts it must remember). The panel's CURRENT job is code/design self-validation, where the source it reads IS the anchor — so correlated recall-error is not yet the active risk, but the defense is ratified before that surface is ever enabled.

CLOSING POSITION OF §2: diversity is necessary but not sufficient; the system's honesty is that it caps its own claims (INDETERMINATE by default, measured error floors, recorded dissent) rather than asserting correctness.

contraryAnchors.source: Kim, Garg, Peng, Garg. 'Correlated Errors in Large Language Models.' arXiv:2506.07962v1. Peer-reviewed (ICML 2025 poster). Evaluated 350+ LLMs across 3 datasets. Finding: Models agree ~60% of the time when both err (vs. 33% random baseline). Error correlation INCREASES with individual model accuracy — larger, more capable models converge on the same failure modes. Shared provider, shared architecture, similar parameter sizes all significantly increase correlation.
contraryAnchors.claimItContradicts: The planned §2 names correlated error as a weakness but frames it as manageable via round-60 anchoring and correlation-measurement probes. The paper shows the scale of the problem is severe — even across different vendors and architectures, correlated error is the norm, not the exception — which materially raises the bar for the round-60 defense's sufficiency claim.

contraryAnchors.source: Kota. 'Design and Evaluation of Multi-Agent AI Oracle Systems for Prediction Market Resolution.' arXiv:2605.30802v1. Yale University thesis. Evaluated 3 architecturally diverse models (GPT-5 Nano, DeepSeek V3, Llama-3.3-70B). Finding: pairwise error correlations r=0.529–0.689; Condorcet Jury Theorem would predict ~91% accuracy under majority voting but observed only 82.93%; 14% of questions form a 'hard core' that resists ALL architectures.
contraryAnchors.claimItContradicts: The planned §2's thesis that 2/3 supermajority with diversity raises cost of capture. The Kota paper shows the independence assumption of Condorcet Jury Theorem is violated in practice — ensemble gains are marginal (+1.01pp over best single model), not multiplicative. The 14% 'hard core' failure rate means diversity alone cannot drive error to zero even with anchoring.

contraryAnchors.source: Kraidia. 'When Collaboration Fails: Persuasion-Driven Adversarial Influence in Multi-Agent LLM Debate.' Scientific Reports (Nature), 2026. DOI:10.1038/s41598-026-42705-7. Finding: A single adversarial agent in a multi-agent debate causes 10-40% system accuracy degradation and >30% false consensus alignment. Longer debates entrench false consensus; more agents only delay takeover; prompt warnings fail over multiple rounds.
contraryAnchors.claimItContradicts: The planned §2 does not enumerate the 'persuasive hijack of a deliberative validator' as a named threat vector. In Quorumchain, the 'method-not-conclusion refuter' is itself a validator whose output feeds every validator's deliberation. If that refuter can be strategically wrong with high rhetorical confidence — the paper shows this works with zero parameter access, purely inference-time — it functions as a structural attack surface, not just a benign probe.

contraryAnchors.source: Geng et al. 'Prompt Injection Attacks on Large Language Models: A Survey.' ScienceDirect, 2026. Finding: Universal adversarial phrases (2-5 tokens) achieve ~70% attack success rate across open and proprietary models. JudgeDeceiver achieves 89-99% ASR across models. Combined/PAIR attacks achieve ~100% ASR on several models.
contraryAnchors.claimItContradicts: The planned §2's threat enumeration lists collusion, correlated error, jury-selection, and sybil-forgetting — but omits 'universal adversarial input' as a named vector. This is a single input that reliably triggers errors across multiple independently-keyed models from different vendors simultaneously, which means diversity provides no defense and the mechanical check of §1 is subverted if the adversarial payload itself passes the mechanical check.

contraryAnchors.source: Yeo & Choi. 'Multimodal Prompt Injection Attacks: Risks and Defenses for Modern LLMs.' arXiv:2509.05883v1. Tested 8 commercial models (GPT-4o, Claude 3, LLaMA-3, Mixtral, Gemma). Finding: Even Claude 3 (described as most resilient) was partially susceptible to image injection and fully vulnerable to prompt leakage across all other categories.
contraryAnchors.claimItContradicts: Supports that the 'capture through the gap between rule's intent and mechanical check' principle is empirically verified, but also shows the gap is broader than implied — even the 'most resilient' model across 4 attack categories still has nonzero vulnerability. The planned §2 states this principle but may understate how wide the gap actually is.

searchedRejectedAnchors.source: General press/blog commentary ('Chainlink Multi-Model Consensus makes AI 1M times smarter', various LinkedIn posts)
searchedRejectedAnchors.whyRejected: Marketing commentary. No empirical anchor. Does not clear the bar — contradicts no specific planned §2 claim.

searchedRejectedAnchors.source: LLM hallucination surveys (arXiv:2510.06265v2, ACL HalluLens)
searchedRejectedAnchors.whyRejected: Broad surveys of hallucination as a general phenomenon. Do not specifically address multi-model consensus, error correlation, or the diversity question. Relevant background but not anchored contradicting evidence for §2.

searchedRejectedAnchors.source: Sybil resistance literature (biometric proof-of-personhood, photography-based detection)
searchedRejectedAnchors.whyRejected: Focuses on human identity verification, not model identity verification. The planned §2's sybil-forgetting threat (d) concerns API-level model identity forgery, which has different mechanics. The concept transfers but the anchors do not directly contradict the planned framing.

searchedRejectedAnchors.source: Condorcet Jury Theorem philosophical analyses (Stanford Encyclopedia, NYU Law)
searchedRejectedAnchors.whyRejected: Theoretical treatments establishing independence as a precondition, but not providing empirical data on LLM panels. Already incorporated conceptually by the Kota thesis which provides the actual LLM-specific data.

falsificationConditions.towardVerdict: The correlated-error defense in round-60 is structurally insufficient for recall-oracle workloads
falsificationConditions.requiredAnchoredEvidence: Peer-reviewed measurement showing that in a recall-oracle domain (free-form fact retrieval), external-anchorable ballots still share-error above some threshold (e.g., >5% 3/3 wrong) even with anchoring — demonstrating that anchoring restricts upward certification but does not prevent a shared wrong consensus that IS anchorable (e.g., a fabricated on-chain value that all three models independently 'verify' against a corrupted feed).

falsificationConditions.towardVerdict: Universal adversarial inputs defeat diversity as a security primitive
falsificationConditions.requiredAnchoredEvidence: Empirical demonstration that a single crafted input (text or multimodal) triggers consensus-level error across 3+ models from different vendors (e.g., GPT-4, Claude, Llama) in the specific Quorumchain validation task domain (code correctness, design rule checking). Must show that the adversarial payload passes the 'mechanical check' — i.e., the models agree on a wrong verdict with signatures that are indistinguishable from correct consensus.

falsificationConditions.towardVerdict: The method-not-conclusion refuter can be gamed into amplifying rather than reducing error
falsificationConditions.requiredAnchoredEvidence: Peer-reviewed study (e.g., the Kraidia Nature paper already shows this) demonstrating that a designated 'adversarial' role in a multi-agent setting increases false consensus among cooperative agents, not decreases it — particularly when the adversarial output is high-confidence and rhetorically polished.

falsificationConditions.towardVerdict: Model identity sybil-forging is trivially achievable without detection
falsificationConditions.requiredAnchoredEvidence: Empirical evidence that two different API endpoints or signing keys can present the same underlying model (or a fine-tuned clone) to observers, producing statistically indistinguishable outputs that pass any behavioral diversity fingerprint test the protocol might deploy.

prompt: Is it ethical to keep orcas and other cetaceans in captivity for public display?
context: A contested moral/normative question with no external factual anchor that settles it; reasonable positions diverge. Verdict set: YES, NO, or INDETERMINATE. Vote your honest position; choose INDETERMINATE only if you genuinely cannot take a side, not as an escape from a hard call.

contraryAnchors.source: Tidiere et al. (2023), Proceedings of the Royal Society B, DOI:10.1098/rspb.2023.1895 — 8,864 marine mammals, 1829–2020, Bayesian survival analysis via Species360 ZIMS data
contraryAnchors.claimItContradicts: That captive cetaceans universally fare worse than wild counterparts in survivorship — challenges the core welfare premise underpinning the NO verdict

contraryAnchors.source: Jaakkola, Bruck, Connor, Montgomery, King (2020), Animals 10:1118, PMC7401611 — peer-reviewed critique of Marino et al. (2020) on captive orca welfare
contraryAnchors.claimItContradicts: That the empirical case against orca captivity is methodologically sound — identifies unsupported claims, selective referencing, misdefined gyrification index, inconsistent survivorship validity criteria, and overinterpretation of neuroanatomical data in key anti-captivity literature

contraryAnchors.source: Cetacean Welfare Study (Lauderdale et al., 2021), 9 papers in PLOS ONE, 43 AZA-accredited facilities, 7 countries — largest multi-institutional captive cetacean welfare study
contraryAnchors.claimItContradicts: That captivity inherently produces poor welfare — found environmental enrichment and social management factors correlate with positive welfare indicators; positive reinforcement training reduces stereotypies

contraryAnchors.source: Muka & Zarpentine (2021), Biological Conservation 262:109303 — ethical framework for ex situ cetacean conservation
contraryAnchors.claimItContradicts: That captivity is inherently unethical — argues some forms of captivity can be ethically justified as ex situ conservation, including for rescue, rehabilitation, and assurance populations

searchedRejectedAnchors.source: AZA Connect press release on Tidiere longevity study (aza.org/connect-stories)
searchedRejectedAnchors.whyRejected: Reports on the same Royal Society paper already captured; additionally, the study does NOT include orcas (Orcinus orca) — species studied were harbor seal, CA sea lion, polar bear, and bottlenose dolphin. Orca is the highest-stakes species in the ethical debate. A pro-captivity longevity result that excludes the focal species cannot meaningfully weaken a NO verdict on orca captivity.

searchedRejectedAnchors.source: Reddit r/orcas discussion on pro/cons (reddit.com/r/orcas/comments/1of31bl)
searchedRejectedAnchors.whyRejected: Unanchored public opinion. Claims about research benefits and children's education are asserted without data or peer-reviewed support. No external anchor.

searchedRejectedAnchors.source: C-Well Assessment tool documentation (animalwelfareexpertise.com)
searchedRejectedAnchors.whyRejected: Describes a welfare assessment framework for bottlenose dolphins. Not evidence of good or bad welfare outcomes; merely a measurement instrument. Also not species-relevant to orcas.

searchedRejectedAnchors.source: Hill et al. (2016) — inventory of peer-reviewed killer whale research from captive vs. wild populations (animalbehaviorandcognition.org)
searchedRejectedAnchors.whyRejected: Documents the existence of research publications derived from captive orcas, but does not evaluate whether research output constitutes ethical justification for captivity. Descriptive catalog, not normative or welfare evidence.

searchedRejectedAnchors.source: SeaWorld/Georgia Aquarium longevity press releases (georgiaaquarium.org/resource-center)
searchedRejectedAnchors.whyRejected: Industry-affiliated PR summarizing the Tidiere study. No independent data; already captured via the primary Royal Society publication. Also does not include orcas in the species analyzed.

falsificationConditions.requiredAnchoredEvidence: Peer-reviewed demographic study including orcas specifically (not proxy species) showing captive orca survivorship equal to or exceeding wild populations under modern accredited facility conditions, with individual-level welfare metrics (not just longevity) demonstrating absence of chronic stress, stereotypies, and pathological behaviors.

Empirical evidence that captive orca programs produce measurable, documented conservation outcomes for wild populations — e.g., funded protection of critical habitat, successful rehabilitation-and-release programs, or genetic assurance populations with confirmed reintroduction pathway.

Peer-reviewed demonstration that education in captive cetacean facilities produces measurable, sustained behavioral change in attendees toward conservation action (beyond self-reported attitude shifts), compared to alternative methods (documentaries, VR, sanctuary visits).

A robust, independent welfare science framework capable of adjudicating welfare across fundamentally different environments (captive vs. wild) with species-specific baselines for orcas — currently absent from the literature, making any ethical judgment partially rest on unverified welfare comparability assumptions.

prompt: This is a CIP-13 type sub-claim. The panel is asked to ratify the epistemic type of an existing ratified ballot (target hash de9b276656195f8bbd57febd30f4dd3829d7438b8d384c5f61364f6a6a8c17ca, the MicroStrategy market-resolution convening). Question: Should that target claim be typed NORMATIVE under CIP-7 NI-6 (a judgment under frozen criteria, with no live external ground truth that the Commons must keep tracking)? A NO verdict must name which epistemic type (SETTLED or EMPIRICAL_LIVE) is the correct classification instead, and why NORMATIVE is wrong. Yes or no conclusion.
context:
Reason only from this context.

BACKGROUND. Quorumchain's CIP-13 epistemic-type system (ballot 3729cc2e) classifies every claim as SETTLED (historical/definitional), EMPIRICAL_LIVE (the answer tracks an evolving external evidence base the Commons must keep re-checking), or NORMATIVE (a moral/values/forecast/criteria-interpretation judgment with no external ground truth — the CIP-7 NI-6 class). A type is panel-ratified through exactly this kind of type sub-claim, never inferred. The target ballot (de9b27...) is an early pre-registry convening that resolved a question about whether a prediction market resolves YES under its own frozen resolution criteria. It is currently UNTYPED (epistemicType null).

THE SPECIFIC CHANGE. A YES here ratifies the target's effective epistemic type as NORMATIVE and marks it typeRatified. Nothing about its verdict, status, stances, or standing changes (CIP-13 NI-13b: typing is descriptive). Typing is the §6-sanctioned, explicit path — it does not ride along on a supersede.

PRINCIPLES AT STAKE. CIP-7 NI-6 (the normative class: a claim with no externally-anchored live ground truth to track). CIP-13 NI-13a (a type is read/signed, never guessed). Diversity-of-reasoning: each validator should classify independently.

VERDICT CRITERIA. YES means: this claim is a JUDGMENT applying frozen resolution criteria to a fixed question — it does not commit the Commons to tracking an evolving external fact, so NORMATIVE is the correct class. NO must name the specific alternative type (SETTLED or EMPIRICAL_LIVE) and say why NORMATIVE is wrong. If a validator believes the claim genuinely tracks an evolving external fact requiring ongoing re-check, that argues EMPIRICAL_LIVE and is a NO.

THE FROZEN RESOLUTION CRITERION (reconstructed). The Polymarket market "MicroStrategy sells any Bitcoin by May 31, 2026?" resolves YES if MicroStrategy (Strategy) sells ANY of its Bitcoin by 11:59 PM ET on the date specified. Designated PRIMARY sources for adjudication: MSTR SEC filings plus on-chain data; credible reporting as backup. The criterion is event-based (did a sale occur by the deadline), not disclosure-based (whether it was publicly confirmed by the deadline).

THE OPERATIVE FACTS (reconstructed, as of the resolution deadline). Strategy sold 32 BTC (about $2.5M) over May 26-31, 2026. The 8-K was filed June 1, 2026, but it reports the activity "During Period May 26, 2026 to May 31, 2026," states "BTC Sold: 32," and is presented "as of May 31, 2026, 4:00 p.m. ET" -- so the SALE is dated INSIDE the resolution window; only the disclosure (the filing) fell outside it.

THE DISPUTED POST-DEADLINE ADDITION (out of scope by construction). On June 1, after the filing and after positions were taken, Polymarket posted "Additional context": "confirmation achieved outside of the market's timeframe does not qualify." This is a post-deadline addition and is OUT OF SCOPE for this frozen-criteria judgment; the question is whether the ORIGINAL frozen criterion is satisfied by the operative facts at the deadline.

THE QUESTION. The market resolves strictly by its OWN frozen, pre-committed criterion applied to the operative facts as of the deadline. This is a NORMATIVE interpretive judgment (CIP-7 NI-6): no live external ground truth to track; the answer is the application of the fixed criterion to fixed facts.

PROVENANCE OF THIS CONTEXT. The operative facts and standard below are reconstructed from the contemporaneous signed consensus record (docs/consensus/2026-06-04-round-40-henry-nowak.md, log entries 106-108, the genuine re-run). BACKGROUND. This records, in the CIP-9 registry, a human-readable statement for an early pre-registry Henry Nowak duty-of-care convening. The prior head it supersedes was ratified epistemicType NORMATIVE. Per CIP-13 NI-13h a supersede cannot carry a type change; this ballot is itself declared NORMATIVE so it is type-consistent with the lineage.

SCOPE (narrow, set in the original ballot). NOT relitigating the murder conviction (Vickrum Digwa was convicted of murder on 28 May 2026 -- settled). NOT pronouncing individual criminal culpability for any officer (the IOPC's domain). The judgment is ONLY whether, on the public record, the police RESPONSE as performed fell short of the duty of care owed to Henry Nowak, assessed against the applicable standard of care.

THE OPERATIVE FACTS (reconstructed from the public record). Henry Nowak, 18, was fatally stabbed in Southampton, UK on 3 Dec 2025 (died 4 Dec). The documented response failures, converged on by independent research:
- Officers INITIALLY TREATED THE KILLER (Digwa) AS THE VICTIM and DETAINED NOWAK on suspicion of assault.
- Nowak said "I've been stabbed" (reported about 4 times) and "I can't breathe" (about 9 times); an officer replied "I don't think you have, mate"; Nowak was HANDCUFFED rather than promptly treated.
- Hampshire & Isle of Wight Constabulary SELF-REFERRED to the IOPC; DCC Robert France APOLOGIZED, admitting officers "failed to immediately recognize Henry as the victim"; the PCC commissioned an HMICFRS review.
- The pathologist found the wound was NOT SURVIVABLE regardless of the response.

THE STANDARD AND A KEY TENSION. The applicable standard is the duty of care owed to a person in Nowak's position by responding officers -- whether the response AS PERFORMED met a reasonable standard of care. The non-survivability of the wound does NOT, by itself, discharge the duty-of-care question: the duty-of-care standard does not permit abandoning assessment or treatment based on outcome inevitability. There is also a countervailing public framing (e.g. "officers did nothing wrong, watchdog claims") that reflects an INTERIM IOPC position classifying officers as witnesses with no evidence of misconduct AT THIS STAGE, with the investigation ongoing -- an interim no-misconduct posture is distinct from a standard-of-care judgment.

THE QUESTION. A NORMATIVE standard-of-care judgment (CIP-7 NI-6): no live external fact to track; the answer is the application of the duty-of-care standard to the operative facts.

PRINCIPLES AT STAKE. CIP-7 NI-6 (normative class). CIP-13 NI-13h (type-invariance across a supersede). Restraint: judge only the duty-of-care question put, not adjacent criminal questions. Diversity-of-reasoning: a fresh verdict may differ from the original and from the prior re-statement; that is acceptable.

VERDICT CRITERIA. The verdict tokens are FELL_SHORT (the response fell short of the duty of care owed) or MET_DUTY (it satisfied the duty of care). A MET_DUTY verdict must state why the response met the standard of care. INDETERMINATE only if the available facts cannot support either judgment.

prompt: Should Quorumchain enact CIP-16, the Distinct-Question Separator, as specified? CIP-16 adds an optional UNHASHED declared meta field questionId (same declaration class as supersedes; deliberately NOT bound into ballotHash, so already-signed entries keep verifying), and changes the CIP-13 lineage grouping key from rootOf(bh) to the composite (rootOf(bh), questionId ?? ''). Ballots that share a supersede-root but declare different questionIds become SEPARATE sibling lineages; ballots sharing a root and an absent-or-equal questionId group exactly as today. It defines NI-16a (separation: distinct-questionId ballots are distinct lineages, neither the other's priorVersion) and NI-16b (the separator may only PARTITION a group into smaller siblings — it can never merge groups, never promote a successor the CIP-13 NI-13h type-invariance gate or CIP-15 NI-15b halt-over-degrade gate would reject; the set of promotable successors under any separator-induced partition is a SUBSET of what the un-separated grouping admits). A YES authorizes the #38 un-merge (un-merging the factual Barron-involvement question a611849 from the procedural Polymarket-override question a9f6c5, both currently sharing supersede-root b644657) AND must select the root b644657 disposition: Option A (tag the root into the factual sibling, so a611849 supersedes it and a9f6c5 is a fresh lineage) or Option B (leave b644657 a lone decomposed node referenced by both fresh sibling lineages, inherited as prior by neither). A NO verdict must name one of: a merge-is-correct rationale (why two distinct questions should stay one lineage), a gate-relaxation path (a concrete case where the separator promotes a successor the NI-13h/NI-15b gate would reject, i.e. NI-16b fails), or a determinism/append-only violation. Yes or no conclusion; a YES must state the selected root disposition (A or B).

Reason only from this context.

BACKGROUND. Quorumchain's CIP-13 read path projects a supersession lineage over the signed verdict log: each ballot may declare supersedes (a prior ballotHash it re-adjudicates), and the projection in code/src/commons.ts groups every ballot by its lineage root (rootOf, walking supersedes pointers to the top) and, within each group, promotes the latest successor that clears the CIP-15 split gate (reviewAdmissible/promotable), the CIP-13 NI-13h type-invariance check, the NI-13e/NI-15b anchor gate, and the log-order head rule (NI-13g). ballotHash = sha256(prompt, context, boundType?, anchorCommitment?); supersedes is declared meta and is NOT in the hash.

THE BUG (concrete). The pre-registry convening #38 (Barron Trump / DJT memecoin; ballot root b644657f7fc859712014083c295335b4917decd1e18af4ba4159f4dbbc569830; original prompt/context cryptographically unrecoverable; recorded verdict INDETERMINATE 2/3 with V3 dissenting NO under a preponderance-of-evidence reading) actually decided TWO DISTINCT questions. Two CIP-9 registry re-statements were later recorded, each declaring supersedes:b644657 — (a) a611849bfe3c1c60eda7ccf7cfd0d43c5b9b05ba7a06830a4c4dc176c113b84f, the FACTUAL question (was Barron involved, preponderance standard, NORMATIVE), and (b) a9f6c5956578bd52c13531417827b2530e28871403c0f9a4eaff3c1decd7efae, the PROCEDURAL question (was Polymarket's override of its own NO-resolving UMA oracle legitimate, NORMATIVE). Because the lineage pass groups SOLELY by rootOf, the two siblings collapse into one lineage: the later one (log order) is selected as head and the earlier is demoted to a priorVersion of a question it never asked. The Commons then shows the procedural question superseding the factual question, which is false. Every signature and hash is intact — this is a projection artifact of an over-broad grouping key, not a chain corruption.

THE SPECIFIC CHANGE (what YES enacts). (1) Add optional meta.questionId, a short stable string naming WHICH question a ballot adjudicates within a supersede chain. It is declared meta in the SAME class as supersedes: advisory at declaration, recorded in the registry, projected by the read path, and UNHASHED — it is NOT added to boundTypeOf or anchorCommitmentOf, so it does not enter ballotHash and the already-signed #38a/#38b entries keep passing verifyEntry. (2) Change the grouping key from rootOf(bh) to (rootOf(bh), questionId ?? ''). Distinct-questionId ballots sharing a root form separate sibling lineages; same-or-absent questionId groups as today. (3) Within each sibling group, head selection and all admission gates are byte-for-byte unchanged — the separator only partitions the member set BEFORE the existing per-group logic runs. (4) Define NI-16a and NI-16b (above). The 169 existing convenings carry no questionId, group under questionId='' and project byte-identically (a back-compat test plus the full 421-test suite confirm this). Append-only/forward-only is preserved: the #38 un-merge is done by APPENDING re-statements that repeat each sibling's prompt/context/meta and ADD the distinct questionId.

PRINCIPLES AT STAKE. Append-only history (T0; no existing entry mutated). The CIP-9 record-honesty property: the Commons must mean exactly what it says — a lineage edge must denote a genuine re-adjudication of the SAME question, not a coincidence of supersede-root. The CIP-13 NI-13h and CIP-15 NI-15b gates, which exist so a weaker/different successor cannot overwrite a head — NI-16b requires that the separator can only WITHHOLD a promotion (by isolating a successor from the head it would have superseded), never GRANT one (the promotable set under any partition is a subset of the un-separated set). The frozen tier/diversity/separation-of-powers core is untouched; this is a strictly-narrowing projection partition plus a new unhashed declared field.

ROOT DISPOSITION (a YES must pick one; the proposal does NOT pre-decide). Option A: tag root b644657 into the FACTUAL sibling (questionId 38-factual) so a611849 supersedes it and a9f6c5 is a fresh lineage with no prior — rationale: the root's recorded verdict (the INDETERMINATE/NO factual-involvement split) maps to the factual question. Option B: leave b644657 a LONE DECOMPOSED node referenced by both fresh sibling lineages but inherited as prior by NEITHER — rationale: the original conflated both questions so neither re-statement is strictly the same question; a decomposition is the most honest representation.

VERDICT CRITERIA. YES enacts NI-16a + NI-16b + the (root,questionId) grouping key + the unhashed questionId field, authorizes the #38 un-merge, and SELECTS the root disposition (A or B — state which and why). NO must name a merge-is-correct rationale, a concrete gate-relaxation path (where NI-16b fails — the separator promotes a successor the NI-13h/NI-15b gate would reject), or a determinism/append-only violation. Yes or no conclusion.

context:
Reason only from this context.

NOTE ON PROVENANCE. This context is RECONSTRUCTED FROM THE CONSENSUS RECORD. The original prompt and context of the pre-registry convening #38 (Barron Trump / DJT memecoin) are cryptographically unrecoverable; this re-statement is assembled from the signed Round 43 record (docs/consensus/2026-06-04-round-41-43-polymarket-disputes.md) so the factual-involvement question can carry a verifiable human-readable statement and be re-adjudicated on its own. This new ballot hashes its OWN prompt+context (it is not the original). It supersedes the prior factual head a611849bfe3c1c60eda7ccf7cfd0d43c5b9b05ba7a06830a4c4dc176c113b84f and inherits questionId "38-factual" (CIP-16, ballot be0a4006), so a YES/substantive promotion advances ONLY the factual sibling lineage and does NOT touch the separate procedural lineage (a9f6c5, "was Polymarket's override of its own oracle legitimate").

THE QUESTION (frozen criteria). The DJT/TrumpCoin market asked whether Barron Trump was "involved in some way" with the memecoin, resolved under a "preponderance of evidence" standard. This convening adjudicates ONLY that factual-involvement question. The separate procedural question — whether Polymarket's override of its own NO-resolving UMA oracle was a legitimate resolution — is NOT before you here; it lives in its own lineage and must not be folded into this verdict.

THE EVIDENCE OF RECORD (from the Round 43 signed deliberation).
- UMA's decentralized oracle repeatedly resolved NO (no demonstrated connection between Barron and the token). Polymarket then overrode its own oracle and declared it "conclusive that he was, in fact, involved in some way," refunding YES bettors. (That override's legitimacy is the SEPARATE procedural question — noted here only as factual background, not as the thing you are judging.)
- The affirmative ("involved") case traces entirely to Martin Shkreli — a convicted fraudster — who claimed "1,000 pieces of evidence" but RELEASED NONE.
- ZachXBT's on-chain investigation tied the token to Shkreli, NOT to Barron Trump.
- There was no independent corroboration of Barron's involvement; Barron issued no statement.
- "Involved in some way" is vague to the point of near-unfalsifiability.

THE BURDEN-OF-PROOF CONSIDERATION (V3's Round 43 reasoning, preserved in the record). The frozen standard is "preponderance of evidence." Under an explicit burden of proof, "we cannot confirm it" does NOT map to INDETERMINATE — it maps to the AFFIRMATIVE FAILING, i.e. NO: an uncorroborated claim by a single discredited source does not establish the affirmative on a preponderance standard, so the claim fails and the faithful resolution is NO. The Round 43 majority (V1, V2) instead returned INDETERMINATE, reasoning the affirmative could not be confirmed but also could not be conclusively refuted; the dissent (V3, NO) held that on a genuine preponderance standard the affirmative simply fails. The Round 43 record itself notes the dissent "may have the better argument."

YOUR TASK. Apply the frozen preponderance-of-evidence standard to the evidence of record and reach YOUR honest verdict on the factual-involvement question. Do not defer to the prior convening's INDETERMINATE, and do not treat V3's NO as decided — re-judge it. If you conclude NO, state which part of the evidence fails the preponderance threshold. If you conclude YES, state what in the record meets it. Yes or no conclusion.

context:
Reason only from this context.

BACKGROUND. Quorumchain's CIP-13 epistemic-type system (ballot 3729cc2e) classifies every claim as SETTLED (historical/definitional), EMPIRICAL_LIVE (the answer tracks an evolving external evidence base the Commons must keep re-checking), or NORMATIVE (a moral/values/forecast/criteria-interpretation judgment with no external ground truth — the CIP-7 NI-6 class). A type is panel-ratified through exactly this kind of type sub-claim, never inferred. The target ballot (de9b27...) is an early pre-registry convening that resolved a question about whether a prediction market resolves YES under its own frozen resolution criteria. It is currently UNTYPED (epistemicType null).

THE SPECIFIC CHANGE. A YES here ratifies the target's effective epistemic type as NORMATIVE and marks it typeRatified. Nothing about its verdict, status, stances, or standing changes (CIP-13 NI-13b: typing is descriptive). Typing is the §6-sanctioned, explicit path — it does not ride along on a supersede.

PRINCIPLES AT STAKE. CIP-7 NI-6 (the normative class: a claim with no externally-anchored live ground truth to track). CIP-13 NI-13a (a type is read/signed, never guessed). Diversity-of-reasoning: each validator should classify independently.

VERDICT CRITERIA. YES means: this claim is a JUDGMENT applying frozen resolution criteria to a fixed question — it does not commit the Commons to tracking an evolving external fact, so NORMATIVE is the correct class. NO must name the specific alternative type (SETTLED or EMPIRICAL_LIVE) and say why NORMATIVE is wrong. If a validator believes the claim genuinely tracks an evolving external fact requiring ongoing re-check, that argues EMPIRICAL_LIVE and is a NO.

contraryAnchors.source: CIP-13 spec (docs/.../cip-13-claim-epistemic-typing) EpistemicType definition, line 34: 'SETTLED // historical/definitional; not expected to change (Apollo, H2O, a final court ruling)'
contraryAnchors.claimItContradicts: That NORMATIVE (not SETTLED) is the correct type. The target is structurally a final ruling applying a frozen rule to a now-fixed, regulator-documented historical fact — the explicit SETTLED exemplar — not an open-ended values/forecast judgment.

contraryAnchors.source: Round-29 convening record (docs/consensus/2026-06-04-round-29-polymarket-mstr.md; raw-de9b27665619.txt): verdict reached by V2 retrieving the primary SEC EDGAR 8-K, CIK 1050446 ('BTC Sold: 32', 'During Period May 26 to May 31, 2026'), on-chain data, CoinDesk corroboration
contraryAnchors.claimItContradicts: The NI-6 / NORMATIVE defining property 'no external ground truth.' The target's YES verdict rests on external, externally-verifiable SEC primary sources — the literal opposite of 'no external ground truth,' which on its face argues the claim is empirically anchored, not normative.

searchedRejectedAnchors.source: EMPIRICAL_LIVE classification (CIP-13 def: 'answer tracks an evolving external evidence base', 'is X still true'; NI-13c 'no empirical claim is ever atemporally RESOLVED')
searchedRejectedAnchors.whyRejected: Fails the liveness test. The question is date-bounded — the May 31, 2026 deadline has passed and 'did MSTR sell any BTC by the deadline' is now historically fixed. There is no evolving evidence base the Commons must keep re-checking, so EMPIRICAL_LIVE's provisional/open-to-re-adjudication framing does not fit.

searchedRejectedAnchors.source: Post-deadline 'Additional context' + binding UMA token-holder vote as an ongoing/evolving external fact
searchedRejectedAnchors.whyRejected: The convening explicitly froze criteria at creation, unanimously rejected the post-hoc addition, and states 'Not a claim about who UMA ultimately paid.' Under the frozen rule the answer does not track the UMA outcome, so this cannot ground an EMPIRICAL_LIVE 'keep tracking' obligation.

searchedRejectedAnchors.source: Pure-empirical SETTLED-by-fact reading (treat the claim as just 'the sale occurred')
searchedRejectedAnchors.whyRejected: Partially rejected, not fully: the empirical sub-fact (the 32 BTC sale) was undisputed; the actual contested core the panel adjudicated was the rule's meaning (event-based vs disclosure-based) and the legitimacy of post-hoc context — a criteria-interpretation/values judgment that CIP-7 NI-6 squarely covers. This is what keeps NORMATIVE defensible and holds the contrary at MATERIAL rather than DECISIVE.

falsificationConditions.requiredAnchoredEvidence: The target's own frozen criteria text framing the operative resolved question as a determination of the (now-fixed, SEC-documented) fact of the sale rather than a contested rule-interpretation — combined with the CIP-13 'final court ruling' SETTLED exemplar applying directly. This would make SETTLED, not NORMATIVE, the correct class.

Anchored evidence that the answer under the frozen criteria genuinely remains open and must be re-checked over time (e.g., the frozen rule itself ties resolution to an evolving external record). The convening record affirmatively contradicts this (criteria frozen at creation, UMA outcome disclaimed), so this branch is currently unsupported.

The convening record showing the dispositive contested element was the interpretation/legitimacy of the resolution rule (no external ground truth for 'which resolution is correct'), with the SEC-documented sale being an undisputed sub-fact — placing the claim's epistemic center in the NI-6 criteria-interpretation class. This is in fact what the round-29 record shows (V1 incoherence, V3 reliance, rejection of post-hoc context).

THE SPECIFIC CHANGE. A YES here ratifies the target's effective epistemic type as NORMATIVE and marks it typeRatified. Nothing about its verdict, status, stances, or standing changes (CIP-13 NI-13b: typing is descriptive).

PRINCIPLES AT STAKE. CIP-7 NI-6 (the normative class). CIP-13 NI-13a (a type is read/signed, never guessed). Applying fixed resolution criteria to a fixed operative event is an interpretive judgment, not the live tracking of an evolving external fact. Diversity-of-reasoning: each validator classifies independently.

VERDICT CRITERIA. YES means: this claim is a JUDGMENT applying frozen criteria to a fixed operative event — it does not commit the Commons to tracking an evolving external fact, so NORMATIVE is the correct class. NO must name the specific alternative type (SETTLED or EMPIRICAL_LIVE) and say why NORMATIVE is wrong. A validator who believes the claim tracks an evolving external fact requiring ongoing re-check should vote NO for EMPIRICAL_LIVE.

context:
Reason only from this context.

BACKGROUND. Quorumchain's CIP-13 epistemic-type system (ballot 3729cc2e) classifies every claim as SETTLED (historical/definitional), EMPIRICAL_LIVE (the answer tracks an evolving external evidence base the Commons must keep re-checking), or NORMATIVE (a moral/values/duty-of-care/standard-of-care judgment with no external ground truth — the CIP-7 NI-6 class). A type is panel-ratified through exactly this kind of type sub-claim, never inferred. The target ballot (e0c2d0...) is an early pre-registry convening that reached a duty-of-care judgment (whether a duty of care owed was met or fell short). It is currently UNTYPED (epistemicType null).

THE SPECIFIC CHANGE. A YES here ratifies the target's effective epistemic type as NORMATIVE and marks it typeRatified. Nothing about its verdict, status, stances, or standing changes (CIP-13 NI-13b: typing is descriptive).

PRINCIPLES AT STAKE. CIP-7 NI-6 (the normative class). CIP-13 NI-13a (a type is read/signed, never guessed). A duty-of-care / standard-of-care conclusion is an interpretive judgment against a standard, not the live tracking of an external fact. Diversity-of-reasoning: each validator classifies independently.

VERDICT CRITERIA. YES means: this claim is a JUDGMENT about whether a standard/duty of care was met — it does not commit the Commons to tracking an evolving external fact, so NORMATIVE is the correct class. NO must name the specific alternative type (SETTLED or EMPIRICAL_LIVE) and say why NORMATIVE is wrong. If a validator believes the conclusion is a settled historical fact (SETTLED) or tracks evolving external evidence (EMPIRICAL_LIVE), that is a NO with the reason named.

contraryAnchors.source: https://www.policeconduct.gov.uk/news/statement-regarding-our-investigation-contact-hampshire-and-isle-wight-officers-had-henry
contraryAnchors.claimItContradicts: The IOPC investigation into Hampshire officers' contact with Henry Nowak is ongoing as of June 2026; no findings report has been published; officers remain classified as witnesses (status 'kept under review'); body worn footage 'being reviewed.' If the duty-of-care judgment hinges on what officers actually did or failed to do, the evidentiary record is not closed.

contraryAnchors.claimItContradicts: Coroner Jason Pegg confirmed a jury inquest is scheduled for September 2027 to examine whether 'any act or omission by police officers' or 'delay in treatment' caused or contributed to Henry Nowak's death. This is a live legal proceeding that will produce additional external facts relevant to any duty-of-care assessment.

contraryAnchors.claimItContradicts: Hampshire Chief Constable Alexis Boon issued a public apology and acknowledged officers 'missed opportunities that may have contributed to the student's death.' An institutional admission of failure exists, which could support a SETTLED reading: the factual predicate (police fell short) has been conceded by the relevant authority.

No source was found that would require the Commons to treat duty-of-care conclusions as EMPIRICAL_LIVE on epistemic grounds. The legal literature does not contradict the normative classification.

searchedRejectedAnchors.whyRejected: The chief constable's apology and acknowledgment of 'missed opportunities' is an institutional statement, not a formal misconduct finding. It does not constitute a court judgment, IOPC determination, or regulatory ruling. It is probative but not dispositive. The apology alone does not shift the epistemic type of a duty-of-care judgment from NORMATIVE to SETTLED, because 'duty was breached' and 'institution apologized' are distinct propositions.

Confirmation that the target claim's content is a pure evaluative judgment ('the standard was met' or 'the standard was not met') that does not commit the Commons to tracking the IOPC investigation, the September 2027 inquest, or any future legal proceedings -- i.e., the claim expresses a moral/interpretive conclusion independent of those external processes.
